figures.caption: Figure 2: Frame A paired difference ∆ = gemma4:26b − GPT-5 with 90% bootstrap CIs per tier (and overall, paired across all 30 tasks). The shaded band marks the pre-registered ±10 pp equivalence margin. The 90% CI for A0 sits strictly above the upper margin (open-weight strict superiority); A and the overall comparison have CIs wholly within the margin (TOST equivalence); E has a CI strictly below the lower margin (frontier strict superiority). On B, C, and D the per-tier 90% CI extends past the margin on at least one side, so neither equivalence nor inferiority at ±10 pp is formally established at this sample size.
Figure 6: Wall-clock latency per passed task vs aggregate TCR. The qwen3:32b cell with reasoning enabled (rightmost outlier) is slower per passed task than GPT-5 in this corpus.

Figure 1: The AgentFloor capability ladder. Six tiers (A0–E), each introducing one new cognitive demand. Per-tier TCR shown for gemma4:26b (the Frame A open-weight anchor; highest overall TCR among openweight models in our corpus) and GPT-5. Per-tier-best open-weight cells differ by tier; see Table 2 for the full breakdown.

Table 2: Per-tier TCR with 95% bootstrap CIs. Cells are point estimate% [CI low, CI high]. Bold = best per tier. n = 125 per (model, tier) cell for the SLM corpus; n ≥ 45 for GPT-5. Bootstrap: 10,000 resamples, seed 42.
Table 4: Cost per passed task. Bold = cheapest reliable option (positive pass rate). All costs are shown in USD. Pricing assumptions are given in Section 3.
Table 5: Wall-clock latency per passed task. Bold = fastest reliable option. SLMs run on a Tailscale-served Ollama host (Mac); GPT-5 over the OpenAI API.

Figure 8: Tier-E failure-mode decomposition for GPT-5 (n = 45) and gemma4:26b (n = 125). The two models reach aggregate TCR that is statistically indistinguishable at the pre-registered margin (Section 4.1); their tier-E failure mass is concentrated in different categories. GPT-5: F5 (early resignation) 39%, F5b (plan-without-execute) 24%, F7 18%, F4 8%, F1 0%. gemma4:26b: F4 (step-budget exhausted) 41%, F1 (hallucinated tool) 36%, F5 23%, F5b 0%.

Figure 3: Capability heatmap: 17 models × 6 tiers, cell value is TCR%. Most open-weight models reach high TCR on A0 and A across the 3–26 B range. The B→C transition is the steepest column-step in the corpus. The GPT-5 row tracks the gemma4:26b row closely except on A0 (gemma4 higher) and E (GPT-5 higher).

Figure 7: Targeted interventions on the long-horizon (E) gap and on Qwen3 reasoning mode (tier B). Bars show paired ∆ TCR with 95% bootstrap CIs over (task, variant, run_idx) tuples. Interventions: explicitsubmission system-prompt addition; doubled max_steps budget on D and E; reasoning-mode toggle on Qwen3; plan/execute/submit phase decomposition prompt.

Figure 4: Smallest open-weight model per (tier, threshold) cell whose 95% bootstrap CI lower bound clears the reliability target. A0, A, and B are cleared by sub-5 B models at the 70%–80% targets in this corpus. No model in this corpus clears any threshold in [60, 90] on C, D, or E without intervention.

Figure 5: Cost-per-passed-task vs aggregate TCR. The Pareto frontier in this corpus is occupied by openweight models at every reliability point; GPT-5 sits in the upper right. Pricing follows the locked 2026-04 schedule (Section 3).

Table 1: Frame A: pre-registered paired TOST of gemma4:26b vs GPT-5. ∆ = mean(gemma4:26b) − mean(GPT-5). Equivalence at ±10 pp holds if 90% CI of ∆ is within [−10, +10] pp. Non-inferiority holds if 95% CI lower bound of ∆ is > −10 pp. Frame B Holm passes counts SLMs (out of 16) that pass non-inferiority on this tier under family-wise α = 0.05.
